words.etymology_note: Old English 'beallucas' (testicles); by the 1860s meant 'nonsense'; Sex Pistols 1977.
Old English 'ærs' — cognate with Dutch 'aars', German 'Arsch'.
From Medieval Latin 'Bulgarus' — referring to heretical Bulgarian sect; meaning shifted through centuries.

words.etymology_note: Likely from 'by our Lady'; disputed. Mild UK intensifier.
Contraction of 'God blind me!' — minced oath.
Dates to 14th C Middle English — probably onomatopoeic.
From 'knacker' — a horse slaughterer; slang for testicles since 19th C.
Minced oath — euphemism for 'Christ'.
Stand-in for a stronger s-word; established 20th C.
Euphemism for a stronger f-word; also confection since 19th C.
American euphemism for s-word; 19th C onwards.
Minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.
Originally 'husks of no value' → worthless → mild exclamation.
words.definition: Originally 'husks of no value' → worthless → mild exclamation.

words.etymology_note: From Yiddish 'feh'; popularised 1920s.
Mild exclamation of frustration.
Minced form of 'Christ' via 'cripes'.
Contraction of 'od rot' — mild minced oath.
From Dutch 'pappekak' — soft dung. Polite now, very rude in origin.
Originally 16th C for a frothy drink; meaning 'nonsense' by 17th C.

words.etymology_note: Disputed origin; possibly from Hiram Codd's bottle + 'wallop' (beer).
19th C slang for rubbish; origin disputed.
From violin bows being flimsy and trivial; 17th C.

words.etymology_note: Originally slop fed to pigs; figurative by 18th C.
1920s US slang; possibly from Russian/Yiddish.
Late 18th C, from 'twattle' (prattle).
From Buncombe County, NC — a congressman's 1820 tedious speech.
Mid-18th C; made famous by Dickens' Scrooge.

words.etymology_note: Originally a theatrical device to 'trap claps' from the audience.

words.etymology_note: Scots, late 20th C; origin uncertain — possibly from 'numps' (fool).
1960s UK slang; disputed origin — possibly from 'Walter'.
Originally slang for penis (1940s); meaning 'fool' from 1980s sitcoms.
From 'twit' (to taunt); as 'fool' from 1920s RAF slang.
1980s UK slang from Jim Henson's Muppets; gentle insult.

words.etymology_note: 1920s, from 'nit' (nothing) + wit.

words.etymology_note: Originally northern English for penis; meaning 'fool' from mid-20th C.
Contraction of 'God's wounds' — 16th C oath.
'God's hooks' — referring to nails of the crucifixion.
Euphemism for 'Oh God'; 17th C.
Minced form of 'Christ'; late 19th C.
Medieval: a knight's attendant → rogue by 16th C.

words.etymology_note: Old English 'cnafa' — boy; meaning 'rogue' by 13th C.
17th C alteration of 'rascal'.
Origin disputed; 16th C.
From Italian 'monta in banco' — one who climbs on a bench (to sell fake cures).
19th C US — a shrewd, unprincipled politician.

words.etymology_note: Hiberno-English rendering of 'idiot'.
Softened form of the f-word; popularised by Father Ted.
Scots for 'crazy person'; origin disputed.
Irish slang: 'gob' (mouth) + 'shite'; someone who talks nonsense.
From an Australian racehorse (1920s) that never won; then bird → fool.

words.etymology_note: From the Aboriginal word for a noisy pink cockatoo.

words.etymology_note: AU/NZ for an unrefined person; popularised 1980s.
Strong insult in UK/AU; anatomical origin. Medium in AU, stronger in UK.
words.definition: Strong insult in UK/AU; anatomical origin

words.etymology_note: Battlestar Galactica (1978 / 2004) — invented profanity.
Red Dwarf (1988) — invented profanity.
Farscape (1999) — invented profanity.
Firefly (2002) — fictional minced 'goddamn'.
Derogatory use since 14th C.
Used of women since 17th C — gendered; review case by case.
words.definition: Used of women since 17th C — gendered

words.etymology_note: From Old French 'damner' → to condemn; softened to a general intensifier.
Southern US minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.
Minced form of 'hell'; late 19th C.

1920s US; origin disputed — possibly from Irish surname.
From Bologna sausage; meaning 'nonsense' by 1920s US.
